content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are given an array of length of NNN. They decide to play a game with it.

Initially, Alice randomly shuffles the array, and then Bob takes NNN turns. In each turn, Bob chooses an index which he has not already chosen and then Alice reveals the number on that index to Bob. If this number is either the first number to be chosen or **strictly larger** than previously chosen numbers by Bob then he takes it and adds to his array else he throws it.

You only know the initial unshuffled array and want to know the expected length of Bob's array at the end of the game.

The final expected length can be represented as a fraction of the form PQ\frac{P}{Q}QP​. You are required to print P⋅Q−1 mod 998244353P \cdot Q^{-1} \bmod 998244353P⋅Q−1mod998244353.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase contains two lines of input.
The first line contains NNN the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2,..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​ representing the initial array.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the expected length of Bob's array at the end of the game modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Sum of NNN does not exceeds 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105 over all testcases.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The possible final arrays will be [2,1][2, 1][2,1] or [1,2][1, 2][1,2]. If Bob chooses indexes in the order [2,1][2, 1][2,1] then he will end up with 222 elements in the first case and 111 element in the second case, if he chooses indexes in the order [1,2][1, 2][1,2] then he will end up with 111 element in the first case and 222 elements in the case. Hence his probability ending up with 222 elements is 12\frac{1}{2}21​ and with 111 element is also 12\frac{1}{2}21​. Hence the final answer will be calculated as 2⋅12+1⋅122 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2}2⋅21​+1⋅21​ which is equal to 32\frac{3}{2}23​.
Test case 222: The probability of Bob ending up with all three elements is 16\frac{1}{6}61​, the probability that he ends up with any two elements is 12\frac{1}{2}21​ and the probability that he ends with only a single element is 13\frac{1}{3}31​. Hence the final answer is calculated as 3⋅16+2⋅12+1⋅133 \cdot \frac{1}{6} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{3}3⋅61​+2⋅21​+1⋅31​ which is equal to 116\frac{11}{6}611​.
Test case 333: No matter what index Bob chooses initially he will only end up with 111 element in his final array. Hence the final expected value is equal to 111.
Test case 444: The probability of Bob ending up with all three elements is 000, the probability that he ends up with any two elements is 23\frac{2}{3}32​ and the probability that he ends with only a single element is 13\frac{1}{3}31​. Hence the final answer is calculated as 2⋅23+1⋅132 \cdot \frac{2}{3} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{3}2⋅32​+1⋅31​ which is equal to 53\frac{5}{3}35​.
